summary: DHH argues that most companies' hiring processes are broken, with generic job postings written by people too far removed from the actual work. He criticizes the common claim of 'hiring the best' as nonsense, arguing that most people can do great or mediocre work depending on their environment. Instead, companies should invest time in crafting honest, specific job postings that attract complementary candidates. He shares Basecamp's approach of having team leaders write postings with input from future coworkers.
key_points: Job postings are too often written by HR or executives disconnected from the actual role, resulting in generic template-driven listings
The notion of 'hiring the best' in absolute terms is nonsense — most people's performance depends heavily on their environment
Good job postings require significant time and effort to honestly describe the role, work, and organization
The goal should be expanding the candidate pool by attracting complementary people who might not otherwise apply
Hiring postings should be written by team leaders and reviewed by future coworkers, not outsourced to HR
money_quotes: The disconnect between how many companies claim that they only hire the best and how they try to actually do that is perverse.
The world is full of people who are stuck doing mediocre work in a shitty environment or blessed to do stellar work by virtue of an elevating one. Most people are well capable of doing both!
The only thing that makes sense is to hire the best – defined as most complementary to the organization – person out of the candidates who apply.
Writing a good job posting is hard because it requires you to actually think about what the position entails and how to realistically portray the organization it's within.
The vast majority of potential candidates in this world are not going to apply to your position in any case. The aim of a great job posting is to expand the pool in awareness of that fact.
It's a banal statement that hiring is some of the most important work that an organization does. But that doesn't make it any less true.
key_insight: Great hiring starts with honest, carefully crafted job postings written by the people closest to the work, not with the delusion that you're selecting from an absolute ranking of talent.
